description: Diagnosis of menopause and evidence-based prescribing of hormone replacement therapy.

sba.stem: A 52-year-old woman with an intact uterus and 8 months of amenorrhoea has troublesome vasomotor symptoms. She has no contraindication to hormone therapy and wishes to start HRT.
sba.lead: What is the most appropriate regimen?
sba.rationale: A woman with a uterus who is peri-menopausal (still within 12 months of her last period) should have sequential combined HRT to produce a regular withdrawal bleed. Continuous combined therapy is reserved for post-menopausal women to avoid irregular bleeding.

sba.stem: A 55-year-old woman who had a total abdominal hysterectomy for fibroids requests HRT for vasomotor symptoms.
sba.lead: What is the most appropriate HRT regimen?
options: No HRT is required after hysterectomy
sba.rationale: After hysterectomy there is no need for a progestogen (whose role is endometrial protection), so oestrogen-only HRT is appropriate.

sba.stem: A 49-year-old woman on HRT asks about her breast cancer risk.
sba.lead: Which statement is most accurate for counselling?
options: Oestrogen-only HRT markedly increases breast cancer risk
Combined HRT is associated with a small increased risk that relates to duration of use
Vaginal oestrogen carries the same risk as systemic HRT
There is no association between HRT and breast cancer
sba.rationale: Combined HRT is associated with a small increase in breast cancer risk that increases with duration of use and reduces after stopping. Oestrogen-only HRT carries little or no increased risk. Low-dose vaginal oestrogen is not associated with the systemic risk.
sba.hook: Combined HRT -> small duration-related breast risk.

sba.stem: A 47-year-old woman with vasomotor symptoms and a history of confirmed venous thromboembolism wishes to consider HRT.
sba.lead: What is the most appropriate route of oestrogen?
sba.rationale: Transdermal oestrogen at standard therapeutic doses is not associated with the increased VTE risk seen with oral preparations, so it is preferred in women with VTE risk factors.

sba.stem: A 44-year-old woman reports amenorrhoea for 5 months with hot flushes. She is otherwise well.
sba.lead: What is the most appropriate approach to diagnosis?
options: Diagnose menopause on symptoms alone without blood tests
Check FSH on two occasions 4-6 weeks apart
sba.rationale: In women aged 40-45 with menopausal symptoms, FSH testing (and in those under 40 when POI is suspected) helps confirm the diagnosis, checked twice 4-6 weeks apart. Over 45, menopause is a clinical diagnosis and routine FSH is not needed.
sba.hook: Age 40-45 with symptoms -> FSH x2, 4-6 weeks apart.

emq.instruction: For each woman, select the SINGLE most appropriate regimen. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

stems.stem: A 58-year-old woman, 4 years post-menopausal, with a uterus, wanting a bleed-free regimen for vasomotor symptoms.
stems.rationale: Post-menopausal women with a uterus should have continuous combined HRT, which is bleed-free once established.
stems.hook: Postmenopausal + uterus, no bleed -> continuous combined.

stems.stem: A 60-year-old woman with isolated vaginal dryness and dyspareunia, no vasomotor symptoms, previously treated breast cancer.
stems.rationale: Local vaginal oestrogen treats urogenital atrophy with minimal systemic absorption; non-hormonal options are considered first in breast cancer, but low-dose vaginal oestrogen may be used after specialist discussion.

stems.stem: A 50-year-old woman with a history of oestrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer and severe hot flushes declining any hormonal therapy.
stems.rationale: Where hormonal therapy is contraindicated or declined, a non-hormonal agent such as an SSRI/SNRI is appropriate for vasomotor symptoms.
